questions.question: What is the right mental model for choosing between stdio and Streamable HTTP?
options: stdio for Python servers, HTTP for TypeScript servers
stdio for "this machine" (client spawns the server locally), Streamable HTTP for "over the network" — no cross-over
Always HTTP; stdio is deprecated
stdio for production, HTTP for development
questions.explanation: Local IDE-shaped clients spawn servers via stdio (the child inherits the parent's trust boundary, no auth needed). Remote servers use Streamable HTTP. Tunneling stdio over SSH defeats the purpose — just use Streamable HTTP.

questions.question: Why was the original HTTP+SSE transport replaced by Streamable HTTP?
options: SSE was too fast for most servers
Two endpoints per session were clumsy, some CDNs broke the caches, and WAFs aggressively killed long-lived SSE connections
SSE could not carry JSON
Licensing issues with the SSE specification
questions.explanation: The 2024-11 HTTP+SSE design needed two endpoints and fragile long-lived streams. Streamable HTTP (2025-03-26) collapses to one endpoint; the legacy mode is being removed across major servers in mid-2026.

questions.question: In Streamable HTTP, what do POST, GET, and DELETE to the /mcp endpoint each do?
options: POST creates a tool, GET lists tools, DELETE removes a tool
POST sends a JSON-RPC message, GET opens the long-lived server-to-client SSE stream, DELETE terminates the session
All three send identical JSON-RPC payloads
POST is for auth, GET for data, DELETE is unused
questions.explanation: One endpoint, three verbs: POST carries client requests (response may be JSON or a short SSE stream), GET establishes the channel for server-initiated messages (sampling, notifications), DELETE is the clean shutdown.

questions.question: Why must a local MCP server validate the Origin header?
options: To log which browser the user prefers
A malicious webpage can make the user's browser POST to localhost — DNS-rebinding-style — and an unchecked server would execute those requests
Origin validation is only needed for HTTPS
To enforce per-country licensing
questions.explanation: The browser's same-origin policy doesn't stop a cross-origin POST from reaching localhost:1234/mcp. The 2025-11-25 spec requires an Origin allowlist precisely to defeat this attack on local servers.

questions.question: What are the session-id rules in Streamable HTTP?
options: The client invents any id it likes and the server accepts it
The server assigns a cryptographically random id (128+ bits) on first response; the client echoes it on every request; client-chosen ids are rejected
Session ids are sequential integers for easy debugging
There are no sessions in Streamable HTTP
questions.explanation: The server mints the Mcp-Session-Id and the client echoes it (including on the GET stream and reconnects with last-event-id). Random server-minted ids prevent session-fixation attacks; revocation shows up as a 404 forcing re-initialization.
